lead_signal.headline: Trump administration announces 'Liberation Day' tariff package on April 2, 2026, imposing a baseline 10% tariff on all imports effective April 5 and higher reciprocal rates up to 125% on China effective April 10.
lead_signal.confidence_basis: Direct announcements via Executive Orders 14256 and 14257, corroborated across multiple policy analyses and trackers.

top_developments.title: Liberation Day Executive Orders Signed
top_developments.summary: President Trump signed EOs 14256 and 14257 on April 2, declaring a trade deficit national emergency under IEEPA, imposing 10% baseline tariffs on all imports from April 5 and country-specific reciprocal tariffs from April 9, with China facing up to 145% effective rates including layered duties. This escalates US protectionism to levels unseen since the 1930s, risking global supply chain disruptions, inflation passthrough, and retaliatory measures. Markets face heightened volatility as corporates adjust sourcing and pricing.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Structural policy shift redefining trade regime, with persistent multi-year impacts on global macro flows.

top_developments.title: China Tariffs Spike to 145% with De Minimis Elimination
top_developments.summary: Reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods rise from 84% to 125% atop existing 20% fentanyl and Section 301 duties, ending de minimis exemptions with fees rising to $200 per item by June. This targets e-commerce loopholes and serial offenders, accelerating US-China decoupling. Expect supply shocks in consumer electronics, apparel, and pharma inputs, boosting domestic inflation and pressuring EM growth.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Permanent barrier to bilateral trade, altering long-term investment and production flows.

top_developments.title: Universal 10% Tariff Plus 57 Country Reciprocals
top_developments.summary: Baseline 10% applies to all non-exempt imports from April 5, layered atop existing duties except Mexico/Canada; 11-50% reciprocals hit 57 surplus nations from April 9. Exemptions limited to ag, pharma, inputs in Annex II. Signals broad trade war ignition, with effective US rates nearing 22%, compressing global trade volumes and corporate margins.
top_developments.signal_type_basis: Cyclical shock to trade balances and inflation, with scope for negotiation pauses as seen in prior cycles.

tariff_escalation.rung_basis: Highest post-1930s escalation via IEEPA emergency powers, layering universal baseline on country-specific reciprocals and ending exemptions; prior phases at ~12% average now surpassed.

asset_class_summary.bonds: Sell signal, yields rising on inflation fears
asset_class_summary.tech: Bearish, China supply chain hits and higher input costs
asset_class_summary.energy: Mixed, domestic boost offset by global demand weakness
asset_class_summary.metals: Bearish, trade barriers crimp industrial demand
asset_class_summary.em_equities: Sell, China/EM exposure vulnerable to retaliation
asset_class_summary.crypto: Neutral, tariff noise overshadowed by risk-off flows

weekly_brief_narrative: The macro regime shifts to aggressive US protectionism with the April 2 Liberation Day tariffs, the most sweeping since Smoot-Hawley, invoking IEEPA to justify a 10% universal import duty from April 5 and reciprocals up to 50% on 57 nations, spiking China rates to 145%. Effective US tariffs approach 22%, layering atop fentanyl, Section 301, and auto duties, igniting simultaneous disputes with EU, China, Canada, Mexico. This accelerates decoupling, disrupting $3tn+ annual trade flows, embedding cost-push inflation at 2-4% passthrough to CPI per prior episodes, while compressing global GDP by 0.5-1% via supply shocks. Corporates face margin erosion in tech (semis, assembly), metals (steel/alum), and consumer goods; multinationals like Apple, GM signal sourcing pivots in Q1 earnings. Asset implications: bonds sell off as Fed hike odds rise to 60% for H2; tech/EM equities drop 5-10% near-term on China beta; energy/metals volatile with US reshoring bids clashing demand slump; crypto decoupled but risk-sensitive. Retaliation risks amplify: China eyes rare earths/tech bans, EU steel quotas, per historical tit-for-tat. Central banks trapped—Fed pauses cuts, ECB/BOJ intervene on euro/yen surges. Next week watch: April 5 implementation volatility, USTR negotiation signals, April 9 reciprocal activation, corporate guidance revisions, IMF/WB downside GDP revisions. Escalation hinges on G7 response by April 10.
